segments.title: Define what the research must explain
segments.summary: Start archival research by deciding whether the project needs strategic, tactical, operational, personnel, or construction evidence.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses a study of cruisers to show that a broad subject has to be narrowed into answerable research needs. A project about why ships were built and how they operated needs different records from one tracing every officer's personal history. The initial plan may change once archives reveal new leads, but it gives the search a useful direction.

evidence.note: The talk distinguishes strategic, tactical, operational, personnel, and construction questions.

segments.title: Choose evidence for a story, fact, question, or theory
segments.summary: A search for personal stories needs different collections from a factual inquiry, while testing a theory requires a wider net.
segments.body: The talk separates personal papers and narrative accounts from national and institutional records. It also cautions that proving a theory normally requires broader evidence than answering a tightly framed question. Defining that purpose early helps determine both the archive and the scale of the search.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts story and fact research, then narrow questions and theory testing.

segments.title: Use a strong book to map the sources
segments.summary: A well-sourced scholarly book can lead researchers from its bibliography to staff histories, technical histories, and archive series.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends beginning with a good academic work near the chosen subject, even when it is not an exact match. Its index, source list, and archival citations reveal useful records, published staff histories, and the repositories that hold them. Detailed notes in a bibliography turn earlier scholarship into a practical route into the primary material.

evidence.note: The transcript demonstrates following Friedman and other academic bibliographies into Admiralty and archive sources.

segments.title: Follow journal citations to primary material
segments.summary: Online journals provide another citation trail to the primary sources and repositories used by earlier researchers.
segments.body: The same method works when access begins with JSTOR or other scholarly journals rather than a book. Their notes and citations can identify source collections, archive visits, and earlier documentary work on a topic. This makes published scholarship a discovery tool as well as background reading.

evidence.note: The speaker recommends tracing journal citations to primary sources and archive lists.

segments.title: Search catalogues broadly, then refine
segments.summary: A broad catalogue search for terms such as sloops can reveal National Archives records and holdings at other UK repositories.
segments.body: The demonstration begins with a broad keyword and date range, then uses catalogue filters to distinguish National Archives material from other participating repositories. Search results can expose drawings, photographs, logbooks, correspondence, and unexpected related collections. The point is not to accept every hit, but to learn what records exist and where further investigation may be worthwhile.

evidence.note: A catalogue search for sloops illustrates date filters, other archives, and potentially relevant holdings.

segments.title: Read catalogue results for institutional clues
segments.summary: Catalogue entries can reveal which departments created relevant records, helping researchers refine a naval-history search beyond one obvious series.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses results to identify material from the Admiralty, Colonial Office, Foreign Office, Treasury, and Board of Trade. Those institutional labels suggest both the administrative context of a problem and new filters to apply. A result need not answer the question immediately to improve the next stage of the search.

evidence.note: The transcript explains using departmental origins and dates to refine a catalogue search.

segments.title: Plan document orders and archive visits
segments.summary: Once records are identified, account for digitisation limits, ordering rules, document caps, and the time needed for an archive visit.
segments.body: Some catalogue records cannot be downloaded and require a visit or a requested copy. Dr. Clarke explains the practical distinction between a normal visit and a bulk order from a single series, alongside limits on bookings. Knowing the likely number of documents before travelling turns a catalogue search into a workable research schedule.

evidence.note: The speaker discusses non-digitised records, visits, bulk orders, and booking limits.

segments.title: Contact smaller archives before travelling
segments.summary: A polite enquiry can uncover uncatalogued or recently deposited material and prevent an under-planned research trip.
segments.body: Smaller archives may rely heavily on volunteers, have incomplete online catalogues, or have received collections not yet fully listed. The talk recommends contacting them courteously with the topic and a known reference before travelling. That conversation can reveal whether a day is enough, whether several days are needed, and where it is practical to stay.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke advises contacting archives about material beyond their online listings and planning travel around the likely workload.

segments.title: Pack for practical archive work
segments.summary: Bring reliable note-taking and imaging equipment, backups, power, cash, and a pencil while respecting reading-room rules.
segments.body: The field kit described here includes a laptop or other note-taking method, a capable camera, chargers, a backup storage device, and cash for archives that still charge for services. A pencil is essential because pens can damage original documents, while food and drink usually stay outside the reading room. These small preparations protect both the research record and the collections being consulted.

evidence.note: The final practical guidance covers equipment, backups, pencils, cameras, and reading-room restrictions.
